origin: Eastern & central North America to the Great Plains
habitat: Dry open woods, glades, rocky slopes & thickets
blurb: A low, mounding shrub with aromatic trifoliate leaves that blaze red-orange in fall; tiny yellow flowers open on the bare twigs before the leaves, followed by fuzzy red berries.
spread: Mounding; layers and suckers into colonies
seasons: Yellow flower clusters on bare stems in early spring; aromatic trilobed leaves through summer; fuzzy red berries; red-orange-purple fall color
wildlife: Early-spring pollinators; berries and cover for birds; butterfly larval host

care.hardiness: Reliably hardy across USDA zones 3–9, so it sails through Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters; a tough, adaptable shrub at home in high-altitude Front Range sun.[1][2][4]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall on a well-drained site; it establishes easily and transplants well. Containerized roots are far less cold-hardy, so keep pots watered and sheltered over their first winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade; the densest habit and most brilliant red-orange-purple fall color develop in full sun.[1][2][4]
care.soil: Unfussy about soil — thrives in poor, dry, rocky, sandy or heavy clay ground and tolerates acidic through alkaline conditions; it only resents wet, poorly drained sites.[1][2]
care.water: Drought-tolerant once established, needing little to no supplemental water in our semi-arid climate; give occasional deep soaks only while it is getting going.[1][2][4]
care.spacing: Give it room to ramble — it spreads far wider than tall (to ~6–10 ft) by suckers and layering; space plants 5–8 ft apart for a mass planting or informal groundcover.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grow from seed (hard-coated, with dormancy broken by scarification plus cold-moist stratification), from softwood or root cuttings, or most easily by lifting rooted suckers and soil-layered stem tips off the spreading colony.[1][3]
care.bloom: Tiny yellow catkin-like flowers open on the bare twigs in earliest spring (around April), before the leaves; pollinated female plants then set clusters of fuzzy red berries by late summer that persist into winter.[1][2][5]
care.feeding: Needs no routine feeding in average soil — it evolved on lean, rocky ground, and excess fertility only pushes soft, floppy growth.[2]
care.pruning: Prune right after it flowers in spring: the flower buds form the previous season and open on old wood, so a late-winter cut removes that year's blooms and the berries that follow. It sprouts strongly from the root crown and takes renewal well — thin about a third of the oldest, congested canes to the ground every few years, or cut an overgrown, bare-bottomed thicket low to rejuvenate it, and it resprouts densely. Pull or cut rooted suckers and layered stem tips at the edges to hold its spread.[4][6]
care.maintenance: Very low care beyond containing it — the main annual job is removing suckers and rooting stem tips so the colony doesn't outgrow its space.[2][4]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free; leaf spots, rust, aphids, scale, mites, and cosmetic leaf/nipple galls can appear but rarely warrant treatment.[2][4]
care.harvest: Pick the tart red berries when fully colored in late summer for a lemonade-like drink; they persist on the shrub into winter unless birds strip them first.[3][5]

edible.summary: The fuzzy red berries are edible and tart — traditionally steeped in water to make a tangy pink 'sumac-ade,' like other red-fruited sumacs.[1][3]
edible.parts: The ripe red, hairy drupes are the edible part; a sour coating on the fruit dissolves in water to give the drink its lemonade-like tartness.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Soak or rub the whole berry clusters in cool water (don't boil, which turns it bitter), strain out the fruit and hairs, and sweeten to taste; Native peoples also used the fruit in foods.[1][3]
edible.caution: This is a harmless red-berried sumac, NOT poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix, a white-berried wetland tree) or poison ivy — only the upright RED-fruited sumacs are used for the drink. The aromatic crushed foliage is citrus-scented and non-toxic.[1][3]
